issues.title: Use of delegation_chain term language uses delegating tasks as the core concept
issues.author: saisrungaram-ai

issues.title: action_subject: The specific entity this transaction acts on
issues.author: saisrungaram-ai
issues.body:
**Problem Statement**
A token says what action to perform but not on which specific entity — leaving every service to figure that out independently.

**Simple scenario**
A loan approval token says action: approveLoan. But there are 50 pending loans. Which one? The loan service has to look up whose loan this is, cross-reference the agent's session, and guess.
More importantly: for shared resources (a document multiple agents can access, a shared account), saying resource: approveloan doesnt justify. The token must say this specific loan, for this specific applicant.

**Proposed claim name**: action_subject
"tctx": {
  "action": "approveLoan",
  "action_subject": {
    "entity_type": "loan_application",
    "entity_id": "loan-8821",
    "entity_ref": "alex.turner@acme.com"
  }
}

**A2A examples:**
"entity_ref": "agent:financial-analyst-workload"
"entity_ref": "workload:fraud-detection-service-v2"
"entity_ref": "urn:dataset:fraud-training-v3"
"entity_ref": "urn:device:sensor-prod-042"

**Taxonomy:**
entity_type — what class of thing. 
entity_id — unique ID within that class. 
entity_ref — how to address or resolve it in the target system.

comments.author: ashayraut
comments.body: As this proposal is inside tctx, this will go in https://github.com/oauth-wg/oauth-transaction-tokens/issues.

issues.title: trigger_type: What caused this action to happen
issues.author: saisrungaram-ai
issues.body:
**Problem Statement**
There is no claim to distinguish "a human explicitly asked for this" from "an agent decided this on its own."

**Proposed claim:**

"agentic_ctx": {
  "trigger_type": "delegated"
}

**Taxonomy for different scenarios**
Values for trigger_type:
delegated — the principal in the chain explicitly requested this action
autonomous — the acting agent decided this on its own
scheduled — a time-based trigger
event — an external signal (webhook, queue message)

Token is incomplete without it: Any policy that enforces "agent must not autonomously trigger high-risk actions" cannot be enforced from the token alone without this claim. Audit logs tell you what happened after the fact — trigger_type enables prevention at authorization time.

Behavioral analysis note: trigger_type: autonomous on a sensitive action (like fund transfer) is a direct input to intent and behavior analysis in decision logs. Log it alongside the authorization decision.

issues.title: principal and type : Mandatory in every token, human or A2A
issues.author: saisrungaram-ai
issues.body:
**Why mandatory for both human and A2A:**

Human flow: principal.type = human
A2A flow: principal.type = agent
Scheduled: principal.type = scheduled bot

**Proposed claim**

"principal": {
  "type": "user" 
}

**Taxonomy  values for type:**
type: user | agent | scheduled bot | event

The type alone changes the trust level and applicable policies. Without principal, a consuming service cannot distinguish a token that originated from a human supervisor versus one that originated from an unattended cron job — even if the delegation chain looks identical.

**Token is incomplete without it**: 
Authorization decisions that depend on the nature of the originating authority — which most enterprise policies do — cannot be made correctly without this.

issues.title: client : Mandatory in agentic_ctx
issues.author: saisrungaram-ai
issues.body:
**Problem Statement**
The same agent can be invoked through different interfaces — direct API, MCP tool, workflow engine — and each carries a different trust level, but the token looks identical.

**Simple Scenario**
Your AI assistant has access to your company's HR system.

1. You open the assistant and ask it directly → you trust this call completely
2. An MCP tool inside a third-party app invokes the same assistant → medium trust
3. An unknown webhook triggers the assistant → low trust, needs scrutiny

In all three cases, the agent identity is the same. The agent_type is the same. But how it was invoked determines the appropriate authorization level. Without client, the HR system cannot distinguish these three scenarios.

**Why mandatory, not optional:**
MCP is now a standard interface for agentic systems. The same resource server will receive calls from direct agents, MCP tools, and workflow engines simultaneously. Policies that say "MCP tool calls must be scoped to declared tool IDs" or "workflow engine calls require additional verification" cannot be enforced without knowing the client type.

**Proposed claim**

"agentic_ctx": {
  "client": {
    "type": "mcp_tool",
    "id": "mcp::approve-loan-tool"
  }
}

**Taxonomy for different client types:**

type: agent | mcp_tool | workflow_engine | code_executor | api_gateway

**Token is incomplete without it:** 
As MCP adoption grows, resource servers need to differentiate trust levels by invocation interface — not just by agent identity.

issues.title: Prior art for future revisions
issues.author: GoetzGW
issues.body:
Hi Ashay,

Bjørn Baunbæk and Götz Wehberg from the Gimel Foundation here. This is a constructive prior-art notification for any future revision of draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents (currently -03, January 2026).

The transaction-token shape your draft proposes - short-lived, context-bound tokens minted per agent action with explicit actor and principal claims - is architecturally closer to where the AI-agent-authorization category needs to land than long-lived bearer tokens are. The Gimel Foundation has published a body of open-core specifications on AI-agent authorization that overlaps with at least three specific surfaces in the transaction-tokens-for-agents design space, and we wanted to put the references on the public record for any future revision.

**Three concrete touch-points** (not necessarily limited to these):

1. Actor and principal claims as candidate PoA Credential mapping. The actor and principal claims in your draft directly correspond to the principal-agent model formalized in GiFo-RFC 0115 (Power-of-Attorney Credential Structure). RFC 0115 carries a richer mandate object — principal, agent, scope, delegation chain, expiry, narrowing rules — that the actor / principal pair could be mapped onto in a future companion document or revision, in cases where the calling context needs to carry not just "who is acting on whose behalf" but also "what mandate authorizes that act and to what scope".
2. Multi-hop scope narrowing — already specified in GiFo-RFC 0115 §3.7. Once transaction tokens chain across multiple agent hops (Agent A → Agent B → Resource), the scope-monotonicity question becomes load-bearing. RFC 0115 §3.7 specifies mandatory scope narrowing: a sub-agent's credential can only narrow, never broaden, the parent's scope, and this is enforced cryptographically rather than by IdP policy lookup. This is the surface the broader category also names variously as "secure agent-to-agent delegation" and "agent-to-agent authorization". When that work intersects transaction-tokens-for-agents in a future revision, RFC 0115 §3.7 is published prior art.
3. Runtime validation of extended / per-action OAuth tokens. The token shape your draft proposes — a short-lived OAuth token with extended claims (actor, principal, transaction-bound context) that the resource server validates per action rather than per session — is structurally an extended OAuth token in the sense formalized by GiFo-RFC 0111 (Extended OAuth Tokens, Summer 2025). 

Please note, this is patent-pending technology of Gimel Foundation.

**One request for a future revision** (or a companion document)
A Related Work / Informative Reference entry naming GiFo-RFC 0111 and GiFo-RFC 0115 as the closest published prior-art surfaces for the three touch-points above. 

**What this is currently not**
Not an infringement assertion against draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents-03 or any successor.
Not a request to change the substantive normative content of the draft itself.
Not a contribution.

Specs are at https://github.com/Gimel-Foundation. 

Happy to provide section-level pointers or take a call.

Bjørn Baunbæk and Götz Wehberg, on behalf of Gimel Foundation

comments.author: ashayraut
comments.body:
Please check the latest draft as I don't use Principal anymore. I am reusing fields in Transaction Tokens and introducing agentic_context and multi-agent details. 
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-araut-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents/ This was published a few weeks ago.
